preamble: The close-mid back rounded vowel /o/ can undergo a historical phonetic transformation into the diphthong /oi/. This shift, known as diphthongization or vowel breaking, often occurs when the monophthong is influenced by a neighboring high front vowel, causing the original single vowel sound to split into two, with the second element of the diphthong becoming fronted and raised.
phoneticEffects: Diphthongization, vowel breaking, fronting of the off-glide, raising of the off-glide, assimilation (to a following high front vowel)

examples.note: The vowel /o/ in the root 'boka' (to write) diphthongizes to /oi/ in the derived form 'mboiki' (scribe), influenced by a final /i/.
Similarly, the vowel /o/ in 'koka' (to judge) shifts to /oi/ in 'nkoimi' (judge) due to the influence of a final /i/.

sources: Topping, D. M., Ogo, P. M., & Dungca, B. C. (1975). Chamorro Reference Grammar. University Press of Hawaii.
